mappings.rationale: Directly covers the geographic and cultural scope of this section on the peoples and civilizations of Southeast Asia from prehistoric times to c. 1600.
Buddhism is a central theme throughout the section, from Theravada Buddhism in Myanmar and Cambodia to the influence of Zen and the decline of Buddhism in the region.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Indian cultural influences across Southeast Asia, including the introduction of Hinduism by Indian Brahmins to the Indonesian Archipelago.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the advent of Islam in Malaya and the rise of the sultanate of Malacca (c. 1400-1511).
The section discusses archaeological sources for understanding Southeast Asian prehistory, from the Anyathian Stone Age culture to the temples of Angkor.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the Mongol destruction of Pagan (1287) and the broader impact of Mongol expansion on mainland Southeast Asia.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the arrival of the Portuguese (1543), the Portuguese conquest of Malacca (1511), and early European intrusions into Southeast Asia.
Relevant to the section's coverage of early European intrusions including the Portuguese conquest of Malacca and Spanish arrival in the region.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the diverse ethnic groups, social structures, and cultural interactions across mainland and island Southeast Asia.
The section explicitly discusses historiographic problems in studying Southeast Asian civilizations and the interpretation of archaeological and documentary sources.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the maritime trade routes connecting Southeast Asia with China and India, especially the importance of the Srivijaya kingdom's trade with China.
